sm: The industry standard open protocols for delegated authorization (OAuth 2.0) and federated authentication (OIDC), running on top of HTTPS.
Cryptographic authentication standards enabling passwordless, phishing-resistant public-key logins backed by hardware enclaves.
Google's federated Identity Provider, holding the largest market share (over 70.0%) of social/federated web logins.
Apple's proprietary identity service, mandated for all App Store apps offering third-party social logins, serving as the dominant privacy-centric consumer IdP.
Microsoft's enterprise Identity Provider (formerly Azure AD), dominating B2B SaaS and corporate authentication infrastructure.
Decentralized identity architecture enabling user-controlled identifiers (DIDs) and cryptographically signed, verifiable claims (VCs) without central IdP dependencies.
The European Union's mandated sovereign wallet framework under eIDAS 2.0, requiring all EU member states to provide digital wallets by the end of 2026.
Draft FIDO Alliance standards (Credential Exchange Protocol & Format) defining secure cross-provider credential migration to eliminate passkey ecosystem lock-in.
FIDO's cross-device authentication protocol using Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) for local proximity verification and WebSockets for encrypted data tunneling.
Standardized OAuth-based protocol used by the EU Digital Identity Wallet for secure credential retrieval from trusted issuers.
Standardized protocol used by the EU Digital Identity Wallet for remote credential presentation to relying parties.

stmt: Google is the dominant social login/federated Identity Provider on the consumer web, accounting for over 7.0e1% of all social sign-ins and 6.8e1% of monthly active users using social login on platforms like Auth0/Okta.
fx: Google's share of federated logins drops below 5.0e1% due to Apple/Microsoft or decentralized alternatives.

stmt: Apple secures its market position by requiring all iOS apps offering third-party social logins to also offer 'Sign in with Apple' as a peer option, leveraging App Store review policy to force adoption.
fx: Antitrust rulings or regulatory action force Apple to remove the Sign in with Apple requirement for iOS apps.

stmt: Passkeys have scaled globally to over 5.0e9 active credentials, with Google alone reporting 8.0e8 accounts enabled with passkeys and over 2.5e9 passkey sign-ins, proving the viability of WebAuthn at hyper-scale.
fx: Passkey usage declines or is abandoned in favor of non-WebAuthn protocols by major platforms.

stmt: Under the eIDAS 2.0 regulation, all EU Member States are mandated to provide an EU Digital Identity Wallet to citizens and residents by the end of 2026 (2026-12-31), legally cementing decentralized W3C Verifiable Credentials and SD-JWT formats in European public services and banking.
fx: The EU repeals the eIDAS 2.0 wallet mandate or postpones the deadline past 2028.

stmt: The FIDO Alliance's Credential Exchange Protocol (CXP) and Format (CXF) specifications establish a standardized, secure method for exporting and importing passkeys between credential managers, solving the ecosystem platform lock-in of native OS keychains.
fx: Major platforms (Apple, Google, Microsoft) refuse to implement CXP/CXF in their native keychains, preventing portability.

stmt: The default FIDO hybrid transport (caBLE) relies on central tunnel routing servers controlled by Google (cable.ua5v.com) and Apple (cable.auth.com) to establish WebSocket connections, allowing these platform owners to capture metadata about login sessions.
fx: FIDO Alliance mandates decentralized or local-network peer-to-peer options for hybrid transport tunnel routing.

stmt: The EU Digital Identity Wallet Architecture Reference Framework specifies SD-JWT VC (Selective Disclosure JSON Web Token Verifiable Credential) and ISO/IEC 18013-5 (mDL) as the unified cryptographic formats for legal identity data.
fx: The EU changes the mandated credential format to a blockchain-specific structure or deprecates SD-JWT VC support.

mech: Our OS generates a unique, hardware-locked public-private key pair inside the TPM/Secure Enclave mapped to a custom DID (did:key) to establish local user identity, bypassing external IdPs entirely.

The OS gate acts as a local OIDC identity bridge, accepting external Google/Apple tokens to authenticate, but immediately proxying and converting them to a local sovereign token, isolating the user from cross-site tracking.

Our OS registers as a system-level Credential Provider (via Android Credential Manager API, Windows Hello credential provider, or iOS AutoFill extension) to intercept authentication prompts and inject local sovereign identities.

The OS implementation uses FIDO CXP/CXF protocols to import a user's existing passkeys and passwords from commercial credential managers (e.g. 1Password, Dashlane) directly into our local secure enclave store.

Our OS implements a custom local-network WebSocket relay that overrides default caBLE endpoints, routing CTAP2 messages directly between user devices over a private peer-to-peer tunnel, bypassing Google/Apple servers.

Our OS layer implements OpenID4VCI and OpenID4VP clients to natively request, store, and present official EU identities and QEAAs, allowing users to hold state-issued identity documents inside their sovereign workspace.

q: What is the optimal key recovery and account restoration mechanism for a purely local did:key identity when a user loses access to their physical hardware enclave?
w: Without a user-friendly, non-custodial recovery mechanism, local sovereign identities will remain unacceptable for mainstream users.

q: Will Apple and Google fully implement CXP/CXF export in their native iCloud Keychain and Google Password Manager systems, or will they only support import, restricting outbound portability?
w: Determines if our OS-layer can rely on native FIDO migration pathways, or if it must bypass native keychains via browser extension injection.

q: How will the eIDAS 2.0 wallet interact with non-EU services and operating systems, and does the protocol stack allow an independent client OS to act as a certified wallet issuer or holder?
w: Enables our OS layer to natively host national identity credentials for EU citizens without regulatory or App Store gatekeeper bans.

q: What is the exact mechanism for EUDI Wallet instances to verify the registry and trusted status of a requesting Relying Party (RP) to prevent rogue entities from harvesting personal attributes?
w: Without a secure, decentralized trust list verification protocol, user wallets are vulnerable to targeted attribute harvesting and identity theft.

b: Decentralized identity (DIDs and VCs) is a theoretical web3 concept with no mainstream adoption prospects.
a: Decentralized identity is becoming a regulatory and enterprise reality through government mandates (EU eIDAS 2.0 wallet by late 2026) and corporate SSO extensions (Microsoft Entra), entirely bypassing consumer web-login pathways.

b: Passkeys represent a permanent platform lock-in for Apple, Google, and Microsoft ecosystems due to secure enclave isolation.
a: The FIDO Alliance's CXP/CXF specifications provide a clear, standardized cryptographic path for secure passkey export/import, enabling user portability across different credential managers.

rec: Implement a local network caBLE/Hybrid WebSocket relay server within the OS layer to intercept FIDO cross-device flows and run them over peer-to-peer encrypted channels, removing Google and Apple tunnel server dependency.
fx: Relying party browsers enforce strict certificate pinning for native FIDO tunnel endpoints, blocking local socket redirection.

rec: Integrate a native OpenID4VCI and OpenID4VP protocol client into the OS sovereignty layer to support the storage and cryptographic presentation of SD-JWT VC format digital credentials (such as the EU PID).
fx: EU digital wallets are restricted by regulation to runs on hardware verified exclusively by Apple/Google HSM attestations.

sm: Apple's system extension framework introduced in iOS 17 enabling third-party credential managers to register as first-class, system-integrated passkey providers.
Google's unified credential API introduced in Android 14 supporting native integration of passwords, passkeys, and federated login from third-party providers.
Microsoft's Windows 11 API (introduced Nov 2025) allowing third-party credential managers to register as system-level passkey providers integrated with Windows Hello.
Microsoft's managed verifiable credentials service based on W3C DID and VC standards, used in enterprise, B2B, and education scenarios.
A non-profit foundation backed by major tech and HR corporations providing a decentralized digital career and education credential network based on VCs.

stmt: Major operating systems (iOS 17+, Android 14+, Windows 11 Nov 2025+) provide native APIs enabling third-party credential managers (e.g. 1Password, Bitwarden) to act as first-class, system-level passkey providers, removing platform keychain lock-in.
fx: Operating systems restrict passkey creation and retrieval exclusively to their own keychains, blocking third-party provider registration.

s: Skype Japan Verified ID Case Study
stmt: Skype's integration of Microsoft Entra Verified ID in Japan for customer verification resulted in a 9.0e1% reduction in fraudulent Skype numbers and halved fraud investigation times, demonstrating consumer-facing success of VCs without top-down government mandates.
fx: Fraud rates on Japanese Skype numbers return to pre-Verified ID levels or the integration is deprecated due to low adoption.

stmt: Microsoft Entra Verified ID is used in enterprise employee onboarding (NEC with 2.0e4 users) and education (UK Department for Education Project Titan), demonstrating scaling of W3C DIDs and VCs in private business and institutional applications.
fx: NEC or the UK DfE abandon Verified ID in favor of traditional centralized LDAP/SSO systems.
